title: Risk and protective factors for childhood suicidality: a US population-based study.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Childhood suicidal ideation and behaviours are poorly understood. We examined correlates of suicidality in a US population-based sample of children participating in the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) study. The ABCD study aims to examine trajectories of mental health from childhood to adulthood and collects information on multiple domains, including mental and physical wellbeing, brain imaging, behavioural and cognitive characteristics, and social and family environment. We sought to identify and rank risk and protective factors for childhood suicidal thoughts and behaviours across these multiple domains and evaluate their association with self-agreement and caregiver agreement in reporting suicidality. METHODS: The ABCD sample comprises a cohort of 11â875 children aged 9-10 years. The sociodemographic factors on which the sample was recruited were age, sex, race, socioeconomic status, and urbanicity. Participants were enrolled at 22 sites, the catchment area of which encompassed over 20% of the entire US population in this age group. Multistage sampling was used to ensure both local randomisation and representativeness of sociodemographic variation of the ABCD sample. The data used in this study were accessed from the ABCD Study Curated Annual Release 2.0. Suicidal thoughts and behaviours (suicidality) in each child were evaluated through independent child and caregiver reports based on the computerized Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia for DSM-5 (KSADS-5). We used bootstrapped logistic regression to quantify the association between suicidal ideation and behaviours, with measures of mental and physical wellbeing, behaviour, cognition, and social and family environment in participants from the ABCD study. FINDINGS: Our study sample comprised 7994 unrelated children (mean age 9Â·9 years [SD 0Â·5]; 4234 [53%] male participants) with complete data on child-reported and caregiver-reported suicidal ideas and behaviours. Overall, 673 (8Â·4%) children reported any past or current suicidal ideation, 75 (0Â·9%) had any past or current suicidal plans, and 107 (1Â·3%) had any past or current suicidal attempts. According to caregivers, 650 (8Â·1%) of the children reported any past or current suicidal ideation, 46 (0Â·6%) reported any past or current suicidal plans, and 39 (0Â·5%) reported past or current suicidal attempts. However, inter-informant agreement was low (Cohen's Îº range 0Â·0-0Â·2). Regardless of informant, child psychopathology (odds ratio [OR] 1Â·7-4Â·8, 95% CI 1Â·5-7Â·4) and child-reported family conflict (OR 1Â·4-1Â·8, 95% CI 1Â·1-2Â·5) were the most robust risk factors for suicidality. The risk of child-reported suicidality increased with higher weekend screen use time (OR 1Â·3, 95% CI 1Â·2-1Â·7) and reduced with greater parental supervision and positive school involvement (for both OR 0Â·8, 95% CI 0Â·7-0Â·9). Additionally, caregiver-reported suicidality was positively associated with caregiver educational level (OR 1Â·3, 95% CI 1Â·1-1Â·5) and male sex in children (1Â·5, 1Â·1-2Â·0), and inversely associated with the number of household cohabitants (0Â·8, 0Â·7-1Â·0). INTERPRETATION: We identified risk and protective factors that show robust and generalisable associations with childhood suicidality. These factors provide actionable targets for optimising prevention and intervention strategies, support the need to identify and treat psychopathology in school-age children, and underscore the importance of school and family interventions for childhood suicidality. FUNDING: National Institutes of Health.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (Release 2.0); N=7994 unrelated children, mean age 9.9 years, U.S. population-based sample

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions 'higher weekend screen use time' as a risk factor for suicidality, but full PDF not available to verify if specific smartphone/social media variables are separately measured from other screen time. Generic 'screen use time' without SM/phone specificity evident from abstract.

FT_IC3.evidence: PDF not available; abstract indicates bootstrapped logistic regression was used but cannot verify specific statistical tests and coefficients for smartphone/SM variables

FT_IC4.evidence: PDF not available; abstract shows OR values (e.g., 1.3 for screen use) but cannot verify extraction of specific SM/phone coefficients without full text

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical study using bootstrapped logistic regression to quantify associations between suicidality and multiple risk/protective factors from ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, The Lancet Psychiatry 2020, DOI: 10.1016/s2215-0366(20)30049-3

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Screen use is independent variable, not dependent variable; suicidality is outcome. FT_EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study, examined 7,994 unrelated children from ABCD Study Curated Annual Release 2.0, recruited 9-10 year olds across 22 sites encompassing >20% of US population in age group

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen use time as risk factor for suicidality. Abstract states: 'risk of child-reported suicidality increased with higher weekend screen use time (OR 1.3, 95% CI 1.2-1.7)'. Specific screen time variable measured (weekend usage).

FT_IC3.evidence: Bootstrapped logistic regression quantifying association between suicidal ideation/behaviors and screen use time among other measures. Reported as inferential test with odds ratio and confidence interval.

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratio reported for screen use time: OR 1.3 (95% CI 1.2-1.7) for child-reported suicidality with higher weekend screen use time (Abstract)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Release 2.0 data examining associations between risk/protective factors (including screen use) and childhood suicidality

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in The Lancet Psychiatry, 2020; original research (not preprint or duplicate)

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen use time is the independent variable predicting suicidality outcome, not the dependent variable. FT-EC7 does not apply. Paper includes both demographic factors (sex, parental education, household composition) and clinical/psychological predictors (psychopathology, family conflict).

title: Associations between 24 hour movement behaviours and global cognition in US children: a cross-sectional observational study.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Childhood and adolescence are crucial periods for brain development, and the behaviours during a typical 24 h period contribute to cognitive performance. The Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines for Children and Youth recommend at least 60 min physical activity per day, 2 h or less recreational screen time per day, and 9-11 h sleep per night in children aged 8-11 years. We investigated the relationship between adherence to these recommendations and global cognition. METHODS: In this cross-sectional observational study, we obtained data from the first annual curated release of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, a 10-year longitudinal, observational study. Data were collected from 21 study sites across the USA between Sept 1, 2016, and Sept 15, 2017. The participants were 4524 US children aged 8-11 years from 20 study sites. Exposures of interest were adherence to the physical activity, recreational screen time, and sleep duration guideline recommendations. The primary outcome was global cognition, assessed with the NIH Toolbox (National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD, USA), which we analysed with multivariable linear mixed-effects models to examine the relations with movement behaviour variables. FINDINGS: Complete movement behaviour data were available for 4520 participants. The mean number of guideline recommendations met was 1Â·1 (SD 0Â·9). Overall, 2303 (51%) participants met the sleep recommendation, 1655 (37%) met screen time, and 793 (18%) met the physical activity recommendation. 3190 (71%) participants met at least one recommendation, whereas 216 (5%) of participants met all three recommendations. Global cognition was positively associated with each additional recommendation met (Î²=1Â·44, 95% CI 0Â·82-2Â·07, p<0Â·0001). Compared with meeting none of the recommendations, associations with superior global cognition were found in participants who met all three recommendations (Î²=3Â·89, 95% CI 1Â·43 to 6Â·34, p=0Â·0019), the screen time recommendation only (Î²=4Â·25, 2Â·50-6Â·01, p<0Â·0001), and both the screen time and the sleep recommendations (Î²=5Â·15, 3Â·56-6Â·74, p<0Â·0001). INTERPRETATION: Meeting the 24 h movement recommendations was associated with superior global cognition. These findings highlight the importance of limiting recreational screen time and encouraging healthy sleep to improve cognition in children. FUNDING: National Institutes of Health.

FT_IC1.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract mentions ABCD Study but cannot verify U.S. vs other cohort without full paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: No PDF. Abstract mentions 'recreational screen time' but cannot confirm smartphone/social-media specificity without full text.

FT_IC3.evidence: No PDF available to verify statistical tests reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available to verify numeric coefficients.

FT_IC5.evidence: No PDF to confirm study design.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, The Lancet Child & Adolescent Health, 2018.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Cannot assess without full paper PDF.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study) data from 4520 participants aged 8-11 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure is generic 'recreational screen time' (24-h movement guideline composite) with no phone/SM-specific breakdown. FT-IC2 NOT_MET per prompt rule: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown'

FT_IC3.evidence: Paper does not report inferential tests linking screen time to non-SM outcomes. Only descriptive reporting of guideline adherence rates and global cognition association.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric coefficients reported for screen time specifically (only global cognition β values without breakdown by exposure variable)

FT_IC5.evidence: Study design reports movement behavior guideline adherence associations with cognition, but FT-IC2 fails (not a specific phone/SM exposure)

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article, Lancet Child Adolescent Health, 2018

title: Demographic, psychological, behavioral, and cognitive correlates of BMI in youth: Findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Previous research has implicated demographic, psychological, behavioral, and cognitive variables in the onset and maintenance of pediatric overweight/obesity. No adequately-powered study has simultaneously modeled these variables to assess their relative associations with body mass index (BMI; kg/m2) in a nationally representative sample of youth. METHODS: Multiple machine learning regression approaches were employed to estimate the relative importance of 43 demographic, psychological, behavioral, and cognitive variables previously associated with BMI in youth to elucidate the associations of both fixed (e.g. demographics) and potentially modifiable (e.g. psychological/behavioral) variables with BMI in a diverse representative sample of youth. The primary analyses consisted of 9-10 year olds divided into a training (n = 2724) and test (n = 1123) sets. Secondary analyses were conducted by sex, ethnicity, and race. RESULTS: The full sample model captured 12% of the variance in both the training and test sets, suggesting good generalizability. Stimulant medications and demographic factors were most strongly associated with BMI. Lower attention problems and matrix reasoning (i.e. nonverbal abstract problem solving and inductive reasoning) and higher social problems and screen time were robust positive correlates in the primary analyses and in analyses separated by sex. CONCLUSIONS: Beyond demographics and stimulant use, this study highlights abstract reasoning as an important cognitive variable and reaffirms social problems and screen time as significant correlates of BMI and as modifiable therapeutic targets. Prospective data are needed to understand the predictive power of these variables for BMI gain.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses first wave of ABCD Study data (4524 participants, 9-10 year olds, U.S. sample)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time defined as generic 'average minutes per week a child typically spends on a computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device' — no smartphone/SM-specific variable; composite without breakdown

FT_IC3.evidence: FT_IC2 not met; screen time as aggregate variable only

FT_IC4.evidence: FT_IC2 not met; cannot extract smartphone/SM-specific coefficient

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analyses using machine learning regression (LASSO, ridge, elastic net) predicting BMI from 43 demographic, psychological, behavioral, and cognitive variables

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, Psychological Medicine 2020, DOI: 10.1017/S0033291719001545

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 not met; generic screen time composite without SM-specific breakdown does not qualify

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study, first wave of data released in 2018, recruited 9-10 year olds across 21 US study sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time defined as average minutes per week child spends on computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device (Cited: Methods section, child-report)

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning regression approaches (LASSO, elastic net, ridge, linear regression) with screen time as one predictor among 43 variables with own coefficient reported in LASSO model and Fig. 1

FT_IC4.evidence: Screen time coefficient reported: 1.17 in full sample LASSO model (Fig. 1); also reported as second most significant variable in full sample linear regression with p=7.5x10^-8 (Supplementary Fig. S1)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data using multiple machine learning and traditional regression approaches

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Psychological Medicine, 2020; original research (not preprint/protocol)

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (predictor) predicting BMI outcome, not the dependent variable. FT-EC7 only applies when phone/SM is the dependent variable. Paper includes both demographic and individual psychological/behavioral predictors (e.g., attention problems, social problems, reward sensitivity).
